takeaway I would say is is what we know
from you know our studies with in
collaborating with those investors and
and just our general studies in terms of
you know uh learning about financial
markets. We know that volatility
clusters and obviously volatility is a
derivative of price. So that obviously
must mean gap gap you know gaps up and
down and price must cluster as well. And
so what we built we built our distress
respon and the tools that power it which
are also the exact same tools that power
KISS which we can conclude with later.
um the same tools. They're all the same
tools based on the same concepts, which
is, you know, you want to be in the
markets when it's time to be in the
markets. And then you don't want to be
in the markets when it's not time to be
in the markets. And that's exactly what
Dr. Mo helps investors do. When you go
back and you look at the cumulative
performance of the S&P 500 since January
of 1998, if you're only long the market
when Dr. Mo tells you to be long the
market, which is a trendbased trend
following system, then you would get
131% of the return that you would
otherwise get from being in the market.
So that's 1.31 times whatever the return
of the S&P 500 was when you're in the
market. And so obviously that means, you
know, when you're not in the market, uh,
when Dr. Mo tells you not to be in the
market, the SM is going down. And so
this is how you chop off left tail risk
from the distribution, you basically
want to have some sort of reliable
quantitative risk management signal that
says don't be long here. And just by
just by not being long there and only
being long in this part of the
distribution, you're going to increase
your absolute and riskadjusted returns.
And that's what we've been doing for
years for our clients. aside most for
investors who want a little bit more
context around this market regime now
casting process that is the basis powers
our entire quantitative respendant
signal historically speaking you know
when you're in a bull market so if you
go back and look at a trailing 385 10
20-year basis there's really only two
rophase transitions per year on average
so that means you're in a bull market
you have one rophase transition down to
a material enough correction that
warrants doing something differently in
your portfolio to essentially mitigate
the volatility drag associated with that
potential potential crash because you
don't know if it's going to be a crash
or not in real life. And then two, you
recover back to the prior bull market.
Or if you're in a bare market, it's it's
basically that in reverse. So you're in
a bare market, you have a big enough,
you know, a rally, big and long and
sustained enough rally in the context of
the bare market to warrant uh, you know,
reposition your portfolio to keep your
seat on the buy side or to to avoid, you
know, FOMO uh on the on the on the on
the as retail investor and then

>> Yeah, absolutely.
>> So the the first way I would always
start with anything that's developing
whether it be bullish or bearish is what
are the quantitative risk management
signals saying because that's the
leading edge of our process. leading
edge of our process is what are
institutional investors doing from a
volatility and price perspective to
asset markets that you know sort of
indicates their underlying flows and so
when we go and we look at our global
macrois matrix market now casting
process that's the core model that
powers Kiss and Dr. Mo uh you can sort
of see the nature of the disturbance in
the kitchen mayor. Uh when we talked to
him Tuesday, we were in a uh very high
strength of signal um Goldilocks market
regime. I want to say the strength of
signal back on Tuesday was 86%. It has
since declined to 66% because we're
starting to lose confirming markets. I
want to say Goldilocks was at 29. Now
it's at 27. Reflation was at you know 20
or some or so. Now it's at 17. And so
ultimately what it means is the
probability the the shape of the
distribution is evolving in a manner
that is bearish. The probability
associated with the two risk on market
regimes is declining and the probability
associated with the two risk offmarket
regimes is declining or is increasing.
And so that doesn't necessarily mean
that we're heading for a robo phase
transition. It probably means at the
bare minimum we're headed for a you know
what we talked about earlier 5 to 8%
correction. However, this is the kind of
stuff that should have your antenna from
a fundamental standpoint because it does
suggest that asset markets as a function
of those institutional flows that we're
tracking in the global macro risk matrix
are moving from what would be a
positively skewed return distribution to
something that at the bare minimum looks
Gaussian skewed and the delta shift is
negative and we have to respect that and
that's what KISS and Dr. Mo picked up on
this week in terms of the signals that
they sent to our clients.
>> Okay. And this is great folks. So,
you're actually watching the value of
the model in real time here, right?
which is you're you're seeing um you
know we we we we a few minutes ago we
saw a version of this slide that had
pretty much Goldilocks fully in the
driver's seat and all of a sudden you're

here. Um and of course that's what you
know then informs the model like what I
said is hey no matter how Darius feels

that he trades on what the model tells
him to trade on. Well, this is the model
actually starting to sort of change its
its signal in real time here.
>> 100%. It's all about the shape of the
distribution of probable economic policy
and market outcomes. And that shape of
the distribution is evolving.
